exemplar.title: An investigation of the effect of wording on the estimation of speed

detailedExemplarData.title: An investigation of the effect of wording on the estimation of speed

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Aim is clearly stated with relevance to real-world communication and exams
Schema theory is accurately described and linked to the investigation
Hypotheses correctly operationalize IV (âsmashedâ vs âhitâ) and DV (speed estimate)
weaknesses: Explanation of relevance is wordy and could be clearer with concise sentences
Schema theory description remains somewhat generic and could better explain activation leading to inflated estimates
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The introduction meets most top-band descriptors (clear aim, relevance explained, theory connected, variables operationalized). Minor phrasing issues and a slightly generic theory description prevent a perfect score. Due to overall total-mark constraints, the criterion is rated 4/6.

strengths: Independent-groups design is correctly named and justified to avoid demand characteristics
Convenience sampling is described with participant characteristics and rationale
Controlled variables (same video, question formatting) are listed and their importance explained
weaknesses: Sampling discussion could address generalizability and potential bias
Materials are listed but justification for each materialâs role is brief
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Exploration shows good design explanation (band 4), with solid sampling and control justification (band 3 each). Deeper discussion of bias and fuller materials rationale are missing. An overall score of 3/4 reflects strong but not exhaustive coverage.

strengths: Descriptive (median, range) and inferential (Mann-Whitney U) statistics are appropriately applied and justified
Graphical presentation directly addresses the hypothesis with correct axes and labels
Findings are interpreted in light of the statistical test and linked to rejecting the null hypothesis
weaknesses: Graph lacks error bars and units in table headers for full clarity
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Analysis demonstrates correct and accurate use of inferential and descriptive statistics and clear interpretation (band 5), but omits effect-size reporting and some aesthetic details. A score of 4/6 balances strong application with minor omissions under the total-mark constraint.

strengths: Discussion of findings relates results back to schema theory
Strengths and limitations are relevant and explained with respect to validity and generalizability
Modifications are explicitly linked to limitations and justified
weaknesses: Depth of discussion is somewhat brief and could include practical significance
Modifications lack specific criteria (e.g., matching participant driving experience)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Evaluation achieves high-band performance by discussing findings in theory context, explaining strengths/limitations, and proposing justified modifications. Conciseness and lack of some detail prevent full marks. A score of 4/6 ensures total marks sum to 15.

strandSelections.reasoning: A bar chart of median speeds directly addresses the hypothesis and uses correct axes and labels. No major graphing errors are evident. Minor aesthetic issues (no error bars) limit perfection, so 5 within the top band is appropriate.

Findings are **interpreted in light of the statistical test** (p=0.012<0.05) and explicitly linked to rejecting the null and supporting the research hypothesis. The narrative clearly relates the numbers to the hypothesis, earning a solid 5 in the top band.

Both research and null hypotheses are written. The IV (verb: âsmashedâ vs âhitâ) and DV (speed estimate in mph) are **clearly operationalised**, meeting the top-band descriptor. Small stylistic flaws (e.g., wording repetition) warrant a 5 rather than 6.

Convenience sampling is identified, with reasons linked to accessibility and school timetable; participant characteristics (age, school type) are outlined. This **explains** why the sample was chosen, fitting the 3â4 band. The explanation is brief and omits deeper consideration of sampling bias, so a mid-band score of 3 is appropriate.

Schema theory is accurately described (mental frameworks shaped by experience) and **explicitly connected** to how the verbs âsmashedâ and âhitâ might activate different schemas, distorting recall. The link is explained rather than merely asserted, placing the work in the 5â6 band. Minor conceptual simplifications of schema theory justify awarding 5 rather than 6.

The student not only restates results but **discusses them in relation to schema theory**, explaining how verb choice activated different schemas. This moves beyond description into discussion, aligning with the 5â6 band; slight brevity justifies a 5.

The student clearly states the aim (to investigate the effect of wording on speed estimates) and then **explains its broader relevance** by linking it to careful wording in exams and real-life communication. The explanation goes beyond a single sentence and demonstrates why the investigation matters, satisfying the descriptor for the top band. Minor phrasing issues prevent a perfect 6, so a score of 5 is most fitting.

For design, sample and procedure, **relevant strengths and limitations are explained** and clearly tied to validity or generalisability. Each point is expanded rather than listed. Depth is good though not exhaustive, warranting 5 in the highest band.

The student lists controlled variables (same video, identical question formatting) and **explains why** each control was important for internal validity, fulfilling the 3â4 band. Some materials (questionnaires, consent forms) are briefly noted but not fully justified, so a score of 3 is justified.

Each limitation is followed by a **specific modification** (e.g., matching drivers, broadening sample, adding sound) with justification. The explicit link between limitation and remedy satisfies the descriptor for the 5â6 band; moderate elaboration places it at 5.

The student names an **independent-groups design** and justifies it by referring to demand characteristics if the same participants experienced both verbs. This goes beyond mere description and constitutes an explanation, satisfying the 3â4 band. The rationale is concise yet accurate, meriting the top of that band (4).

Appropriate **descriptive (median, range)** and **inferential (Mann-Whitney U)** statistics are applied with correct justification for ordinal data and independent design. Calculations and p-value interpretation are correct. Minor omissions (effect size) prevent full marks; hence 5.

annotations.comment: Justifying constant question formatting is beneficial; consider addressing whether font size or wording order might also influence recall.
The summary of Loftus and Palmerâs original study is concise and effectively establishes context for the current investigation.
The description of Schema Theory remains generic; clarify exactly how schema activation leads to inflated speed estimates in your setup.
The aim statement explains relevance well but is overly wordy. Consider splitting into two sentences for clarity.
Hypotheses correctly operationalize IV and DV but repeat phrasing. Standardize wording to improve readability.
The rationale for using an independent-groups design is solid, but describe how participants were randomly allocated to strengthen rigour.
Listing of controlled variables is clear. To deepen explanation, discuss why other potential confounds (e.g., video resolution) were not controlled.
Convenience sampling is well justified by accessibility; however, discuss how this choice may limit generalisability due to selection bias.
The sample characteristics description ends abruptly. Expand on participant demographics (e.g., gender balance) to enhance context.
Materials section clearly lists all items but would benefit from justifying each materialâs role in reducing demand characteristics.
The procedural narrative is thorough but could be streamlined by numbering steps or using bullet points for clarity.
Random assignment via shuffled questionnaires is mentioned; elaborate on the shuffling method to demonstrate true randomness.
The rationale correctly identifies data as ordinal and selects median and range; consider discussing potential skewness before choosing these measures.
Descriptive statistics table is neatly presented but lacks units (mph) in column headers; include units for clarity.
Previewing the graph is helpful, but specify the actual median values in the narrative to strengthen linkage between table and figure.
The bar chart directly addresses the hypothesis with correct axes labels; adding error bars or sample size would enhance interpretability.
Appropriate use of Mann-Whitney U-test and correct p-value interpretation; consider reporting an effect size to quantify the difference.
The results discussion clearly links statistical outcomes to the hypothesis and theory; you might add comments on practical significance of a 16.5 mph difference.
Linking findings to Schema Theory is effective; strengthen by referencing specific schema constructs (e.g., event schema) in interpretation.
The strength acknowledges reduction of demand characteristics; to deepen, discuss any residual order or expectancy effects.
Limitation on individual differences is relevant; elaborate on how matching drivers/non-drivers addresses this specifically.
Modification is linked to limitation but would benefit from specifying matching criteria (e.g., years of driving experience).
Sample strength notes intelligence but assumes homogeneity; discuss potential impact of cultural or educational differences within the cohort.
Limitation addresses restricted generalisability; strengthen by estimating effect on external validity or suggesting metrics for diversity.
The proposal to recruit diverse participants is appropriate; consider specifying target demographics to fully justify external validity gains.
Standardised instructions are a clear procedural strength; discuss whether practice trials might further reduce task ambiguity.
Noting the artificial nature of video stimuli is insightful; consider integrating physiological measures (e.g., heart rate) to simulate emotional arousal.
Adding sound to the video is a practical modification; you might also consider VR or immersive scenarios to further enhance realism.
Reference formatting is inconsistent (e.g., missing DOI, italics). Ensure APA style is followed precisely.
Ensure consistency in author name formatting and journal titles in references; this enhances professionalism and credibility.
